ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated seekh kebab entry; A0FKP (lamb meat, minced, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lamb seekh kebab in Singapore is one skewer of 2–3 pieces, weighing approximately 120 g.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Sheek Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010788 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for high-protein minced meat kebab with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb seekh kebab. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous category for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes. Carbohydrate content is 5.24 g/100g; GL per 120 g serving = (30 × 5.24 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 1.9, rounded to 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit lamb seekh kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080 mg/100 g; if consumed, restrict portion to no more than 80 g and avoid additional high-sodium condiments or accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g) and low sugar (2.85 g/100 g) are favourable, but high fat (26.4 g/100 g) may delay gastric emptying and cause prolonged postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb seekh kebab has a low glycaemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it broadly suitable for type 2 diabetes management; however, the high fat content (26.4 g/100 g) warrants post-meal glucose monitoring, and high-GI accompaniments such as naan or white rice should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g), high sodium (1080 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume lamb seekh kebab only occasionally and in small portions (≤80 g), given its high saturated fat, sodium, and cholesterol content, which can raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokines; estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; sodium 1080 mg/100 g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb seekh kebab is not recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high arachidonic acid content from red meat, elevated saturated fat, and very high sodium, all of which promote systemic inflammation and joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Seekh Kebab (also spelled Sheek Kebab or Seekh Kabab) is a popular Indian-origin grilled dish widely available in Singapore's Indian and Mughlai restaurants and hawker stalls. It is made from finely minced lamb blended with aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, green and red chillies, and onion, then moulded onto metal or bamboo skewers and grilled or charcoal-cooked until lightly charred on the outside and juicy within. The texture is firm yet tender, with a smoky, spiced flavour profile. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g), 5.24 g carbohydrate, 2.85 g sugar, 0 g dietary fibre, and 1080 mg sodium. The very high sodium content (1080 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients without strict portion control. The low carbohydrate and low GI profile (estimated GI ~30) make it broadly compatible with type 2 diabetes management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. The high arachidonic acid load from red meat disqualifies it for rheumatoid arthritis patients. It is typically served as a starter or main course at lunch or dinner, often accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. In Singapore, it is a staple at Indian Muslim restaurants and is commonly available in halal-certified establishments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lamb seekh kebab?
Is lamb seekh kebab suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat seekh kebab if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of lamb seekh kebab?
Is seekh kebab high in sodium?
What spices are in lamb seekh kebab?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010788, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Source match type is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes, which reduces overall confidence for the GI component to medium; however, the core nutritional record confidence remains high.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this preparation; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source, consistent with a spiced minced meat product with no plant fibre ingredients in significant quantity. 2. saturated_fat_g estimated at 9.5 g/100 g based on typical lamb mince fatty acid composition (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 17283, ground lamb, cooked); this value is not directly reported by HPB and is documented as an estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific HPB lamb seekh kebab preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) from the University of Sydney GI Database category for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes; no direct GI measurement for lamb seekh kebab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16. 5. Food name standardised to 'Lamb Seekh Kebab' (correct transliteration); input used 'Sheek' which is a common alternate spelling preserved in aliases.
